{{lang|ja|平徳子}} |succession = [[Empress consort of Japan]] |image = Jakkoin by Mizuno Toshikata.jpg |reign = March 6, 1172 – January 1, 1182 |consort = yes |spouse = {{marriage|[[Emperor Takakura]]|1172|1181|end=died}} |issue = [[Emperor Antoku]] |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] |father = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] |mother = [[Taira no Tokiko]] |birth_date = 1155 |birth_place = |death_date = {{death date and age|1214|1|25|1155}} |death_place = Heian-kyō (Kyōto) |date of burial = |place of burial = |}} {{nihongo|'''Taira no Tokuko''' (Taira no Tokushi)|平 徳子||1155 – January 25, 1214}},[[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 333. later known as {{nihongo|'''Kenreimon-in'''|建礼門院|}}, was the daughter of [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[Taira no Tokiko]]. She was [[empress consort]] of [[Emperor Takakura]]. Tokuko was also the last Imperial survivor from the great naval [[battle of Dan-no-ura]].Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' pp. 652-678; [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 211-212. Her life became a compelling narrative which survives as both history and literature.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=978-0-231-13803-1 |pages=148–169}} ==In history== ===Daughter of an emperor=== Tokuko became the adopted daughter of {{nihongo|[[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]|後白河天皇,|''Go-Shirakawa-tennō''}}, the 77th emperor of Japan who reigned from 1155 through 1158. In 1171, when Tokuko was 17, the emperor had abdicated the throne and entered the Buddhist priesthood, taking the Buddhist name of Gyōshin. ===Consort of an emperor=== In 1172, Tokuko was married to Go-Shirakawa's fourth and only surviving son, [[Emperor Takakura]].Titsingh, pp. 188-190; Brown, pp. 326-327; [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki.'' pp. 205-208. Takakura was also her first cousin as both their mothers were half-sisters. The wedding was an arranged one, as to cement the alliance between the two co-fathers-in-law; Go-Shirakawa sponsored Kiyomori's rise as [[Daijō-daijin|Chancellor of the Realm]], while Kiyomori provided military and financial support to Go-Shirakawa. Tokuko and Takakura's son, [[Emperor Antoku|Prince Tokihito]], was born in 1178. Just a year later, Kiyomori launched a [[coup d'état]], sacking his political rivals from their posts, banishing them, and replacing them with his allies and clansmen. He even had Go-Shirakawa imprisoned; at this point they had fallen out of their political alliance.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |page=268,285}} [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|left|The ''Kenrei-mon'' (建礼門), one of the entrance gates to [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]]] In 1180, [[Emperor Takakura]] was forced to abdicate by Kiyomori; Prince Tokihito ascended the throne as [[Emperor Antoku]]. Now an empress dowager, Tokuko received the name Kenrei-mon In.Brown, p. 331; Titsingh, p. 424; Kitagawa, p. 764. In this period, the names of the several gates in the walls surrounding the imperial grounds refer not only to the wall-openings themselves; these names were also used to refer indirectly to a nearby residence of an empress whose husband had abdicated, or as an indirect way of referring to an empress dowager herself. For example, {{nihongo|Kenrei-mon In|建礼門院}}, whose official home, after the abdication and death of Emperor Takakura, was located near the Kenrei Gate. ===Mother of an emperor=== Tokuko, now Kenrei-mon In, was mother to Emperor Antoku. The child emperor reigned from 1180 through 1185.Titsingh, pp. 200-207; Brown, pp. 333-334; Varley, pp. 214-215. ===Survivor of Dan-no-ura=== Arguably, the most difficult moments in Kenreimon-In's life occurred near the close of the battle of Dan-no-ura, which took place near the southern tip of [[Honshū]] in [[Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|Shimonoseki]], [[Yamaguchi prefecture|Yamaguchi]]. * '''''[[Genryaku]] 2''''', on the 24th day of the 3rd month (April 25, 1185): The Taira and the Minamoto clashed for the last time.Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' p. 787; Titsingh, pp. 211-212. The Taira were defeated decisively. Many of the Taira samurai threw themselves into the waves rather than live to see their clan's ultimate defeat at the hands of the Minamoto. Antoku's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], Kiyomori's widow, leapt into the water with the young emperor clasped firmly in her arms.Kitagawa, pp. 676-677. Kenrei-mon In also tried to drown herself; but according to the conventionally accepted accounts, she was pulled out with a [[rake (tool)|rake]] by her long hair.Kitagawa, pp. 678; Titsingh, p. 212. ===Buddhist nun=== This sometime daughter, wife, and mother of emperors became a recluse in her later years. * '''''[[Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 1''''', on the 1st day of the 5th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In took the tonsure at [[Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)|Chōraku-ji]], a branch temple of [[Enryaku-ji]] on [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama]].Kitagawa, pp. 763-765. * '''''Bunji 1''''', on the 30th day of the 9th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In retreated further from the world when she moved to {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院}}, a Buddhist nunnery near the village of {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|lt=Ōhara|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, northeast of the Heian-kyō.Kitagawa, pp. 766-768, 787.[http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html ...Click for link to photos of Jakkō-in and Ōhara] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071024175701/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html |date=2007-10-24 }} * '''''Bunji 2''''', on the 20th day of the 4th month (1186): Gyōshin, the cloistered former-Emperor Go-Shirakawa, visited Kenrei-mon In at her rural retreat in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 769-779, 788. * '''''[[Kenkyū]] 2''''', in the 2nd month (1192): Kenrei-mon In dies in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 780-782, 788. [Although ''The Tale of the Heike'' gives 1192 as the year of Kenrei-mon In's death, the ''Gukanshō'' identifies 1213 as the year of her death -- cf. Brown, p. 333.] This once-pampered great lady is said to have composed this poem in her hermit's hut: {{poemquote| Did I ever dream That I would behold the moon Here on the mountain -- The moon that I used to view In the sky o'er the palace?Kitagawa, p. 772 }} ==In literature== Many stories and works of art depict this period in Japanese history, and it is through these sources that the life of Taira no Tokuko is best known. ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is the most famous of the sources from which we learn about this historical character, although many [[kabuki]] and [[bunraku]] plays reproduce events of the war as well. The central theme of the Heike story—and the mirrored theme of Taira no Tokuko's life story—is a demonstration of the Buddhist law of [[impermanence]]. The theme of impermanence (''mujō'') is captured in the opening passage:
The sound of the Gion Shōja bells echoes the impermanence of all things; the color of the sāla flowers reveals the truth that the prosperous must decline. The proud do not endure, they are like a dream on a spring night; the mighty fall at last, they are as dust before the wind.[[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike,''.
In this and other classic Japanese tales, the central figures are popularly well known, the major events are generally understood, and the stakes as they were understood at the time are conventionally accepted as elements in the foundation of Japanese culture. The accuracy of each of these historical records has become a compelling subject for further study; and some accounts have been shown to withstand close scrutiny, while other presumed “facts” have turned out to be inaccurate.Brown, pp. 385-386. In English-language literature, Tokuko's life and reign are depicted throughout the two-volume historical fiction narrative, ''White as Bone, Red as Blood'', by [[Cerridwen Fallingstar]], published in 2009 and 2011, respectively.[https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578027119 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Fox Sorceress''] (2009), and [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578073552 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Storm God''] (2011). ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichiro Ishida. (1979). ''The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō'.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0}} * Kitagawa, Hiroshi and Bruce T. Tsuchida. (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Palo Alto: [[Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-1803-2}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''. Stanford, California: [[Stanford University Press]]. * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'' (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --Click link to digitized, full-text copy of this book (in French)] * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''"A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa.'' New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Jakkō-in (Ōhara)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090914081019/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 |date=2009-09-14 }} * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105003911/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en |date=2008-01-05 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} revision.text:
{{Redirect|Kenreimonin|the asteroid|5242 Kenreimonin}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty |name = Taira no Tokuko
{{lang|ja|平徳子}} |succession = [[Empress consort of Japan]] |image = Jakkoin by Mizuno Toshikata.jpg |reign = March 6, 1172 – January 1, 1182 |consort = yes |spouse = {{marriage|[[Emperor Takakura]]|1172|1181|end=died}} |issue = [[Emperor Antoku]] |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] |father = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] |mother = [[Taira no Tokiko]] |birth_date = 1155 |birth_place = |death_date = {{death date and age|1214|1|25|1155}} |death_place = Heian-kyō (Kyōto) |date of burial = |place of burial = |}} {{nihongo|'''Taira no Tokuko''' (Taira no Tokushi)|平 徳子||1155 – January 25, 1214}},[[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 333. later known as {{nihongo|'''Kenreimon-in'''|建礼門院|}}, was the daughter of [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[Taira no Tokiko]]. She was [[empress consort]] of [[Emperor Takakura]]. Tokuko was also the last Imperial survivor from the great naval [[battle of Dan-no-ura]].Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' pp. 652-678; [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 211-212. Her life became a compelling narrative which survives as both history and literature.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=978-0-231-13803-1 |pages=148–169}} ==In history== ===Daughter of an emperor=== Tokuko became the adopted daughter of {{nihongo|[[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]|後白河天皇,|''Go-Shirakawa-tennō''}}, the 77th emperor of Japan who reigned from 1155 through 1158. In 1171, when Tokuko was 17, the emperor had abdicated the throne and entered the Buddhist priesthood, taking the Buddhist name of Gyōshin. ===Consort of an emperor=== In 1172, Tokuko was married to Go-Shirakawa's fourth and only surviving son, [[Emperor Takakura]].Titsingh, pp. 188-190; Brown, pp. 326-327; [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki.'' pp. 205-208. Takakura was also her first cousin as both their mothers were half-sisters. The wedding was an arranged one, as to cement the alliance between the two co-fathers-in-law; Go-Shirakawa sponsored Kiyomori's rise as [[Daijō-daijin|Chancellor of the Realm]], while Kiyomori provided military and financial support to Go-Shirakawa. Tokuko and Takakura's son, [[Emperor Antoku|Prince Tokihito]], was born in 1178. Just a year later, Kiyomori launched a [[coup d'état]], sacking his political rivals from their posts, banishing them, and replacing them with his allies and clansmen. He even had Go-Shirakawa imprisoned; at this point they had fallen out of their political alliance.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |page=268,285}} [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|left|The ''Kenrei-mon'' (建礼門), one of the entrance gates to [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]]] In 1180, [[Emperor Takakura]] was forced to abdicate by Kiyomori; Prince Tokihito ascended the throne as [[Emperor Antoku]]. Now an empress dowager, Tokuko received the name Kenrei-mon In.Brown, p. 331; Titsingh, p. 424; Kitagawa, p. 764. In this period, the names of the several gates in the walls surrounding the imperial grounds refer not only to the wall-openings themselves; these names were also used to refer indirectly to a nearby residence of an empress whose husband had abdicated, or as an indirect way of referring to an empress dowager herself. For example, {{nihongo|Kenrei-mon In|建礼門院}}, whose official home, after the abdication and death of Emperor Takakura, was located near the Kenrei Gate. ===Mother of an emperor=== Tokuko, now Kenrei-mon In, was mother to Emperor Antoku. The child emperor reigned from 1180 through 1185.Titsingh, pp. 200-207; Brown, pp. 333-334; Varley, pp. 214-215. ===Survivor of Dan-no-ura=== Arguably, the most difficult moments in Kenreimon-In's life occurred near the close of the battle of Dan-no-ura, which took place near the southern tip of [[Honshū]] in [[Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|Shimonoseki]], [[Yamaguchi prefecture|Yamaguchi]]. * '''''[[Genryaku]] 2''''', on the 24th day of the 3rd month (April 25, 1185): The Taira and the Minamoto clashed for the last time.Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' p. 787; Titsingh, pp. 211-212. The Taira were defeated decisively. Many of the Taira samurai threw themselves into the waves rather than live to see their clan's ultimate defeat at the hands of the Minamoto. Antoku's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], Kiyomori's widow, leapt into the water with the young emperor clasped firmly in her arms.Kitagawa, pp. 676-677. Kenrei-mon In also tried to drown herself; but according to the conventionally accepted accounts, she was pulled out with a [[rake (tool)|rake]] by her long hair.Kitagawa, pp. 678; Titsingh, p. 212. ===Buddhist nun=== This sometime daughter, wife, and mother of emperors became a recluse in her later years. * '''''[[Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 1''''', on the 1st day of the 5th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In took the tonsure at [[Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)|Chōraku-ji]], a branch temple of [[Enryaku-ji]] on [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama]].Kitagawa, pp. 763-765. * '''''Bunji 1''''', on the 30th day of the 9th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In retreated further from the world when she moved to {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院}}, a Buddhist nunnery near the village of {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|lt=Ōhara|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, northeast of the Heian-kyō.Kitagawa, pp. 766-768, 787.[http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html ...Click for link to photos of Jakkō-in and Ōhara] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071024175701/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html |date=2007-10-24 }} * '''''Bunji 2''''', on the 20th day of the 4th month (1186): Gyōshin, the cloistered former-Emperor Go-Shirakawa, visited Kenrei-mon In at her rural retreat in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 769-779, 788. * '''''[[Kenkyū]] 2''''', in the 2nd month (1192): Kenrei-mon In dies in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 780-782, 788. [Although ''The Tale of the Heike'' gives 1192 as the year of Kenrei-mon In's death, the ''Gukanshō'' identifies 1213 as the year of her death -- cf. Brown, p. 333.] This once-pampered great lady is said to have composed this poem in her hermit's hut: {{poemquote| Did I ever dream That I would behold the moon Here on the mountain -- The moon that I used to view In the sky o'er the palace?Kitagawa, p. 772 }} ==In literature== Many stories and works of art depict this period in Japanese history, and it is through these sources that the life of Taira no Tokuko is best known. ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is the most famous of the sources from which we learn about this historical character, although many [[kabuki]] and [[bunraku]] plays reproduce events of the war as well. The central theme of the Heike story—and the mirrored theme of Taira no Tokuko's life story—is a demonstration of the Buddhist law of [[impermanence]]. The theme of impermanence (''mujō'') is captured in the opening passage:
The sound of the Gion Shōja bells echoes the impermanence of all things; the color of the sāla flowers reveals the truth that the prosperous must decline. The proud do not endure, they are like a dream on a spring night; the mighty fall at last, they are as dust before the wind.[[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike,''.
In this and other classic Japanese tales, the central figures are popularly well known, the major events are generally understood, and the stakes as they were understood at the time are conventionally accepted as elements in the foundation of Japanese culture. The accuracy of each of these historical records has become a compelling subject for further study; and some accounts have been shown to withstand close scrutiny, while other presumed “facts” have turned out to be inaccurate.Brown, pp. 385-386. In English-language literature, Tokuko's life and reign are depicted throughout the two-volume historical fiction narrative, ''White as Bone, Red as Blood'', by [[Cerridwen Fallingstar]], published in 2009 and 2011, respectively.[https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578027119 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Fox Sorceress''] (2009), and [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578073552 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Storm God''] (2011). ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichiro Ishida. (1979). ''The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō'.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0}} * Kitagawa, Hiroshi and Bruce T. Tsuchida. (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Palo Alto: [[Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-1803-2}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''. Stanford, California: [[Stanford University Press]]. * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'' (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --Click link to digitized, full-text copy of this book (in French)] * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''"A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa.'' New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Jakkō-in (Ōhara)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090914081019/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 |date=2009-09-14 }} * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105003911/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en |date=2008-01-05 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before= [[Fujiwara no Ikushi]] | title=[[Japanese empresses|Empress consort of Japan]] | after=[[Princess Sukeko]] | years=1172–1182}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taira no Tokuko]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taira no Tokuko]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:転送]] {{Redirect|Kenreimonin|the asteroid|5242 Kenreimonin}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty |name = Taira no Tokuko
{{lang|ja|平徳子}} |succession = [[Empress consort of Japan]] |image = Jakkoin by Mizuno Toshikata.jpg |reign = March 6, 1172 – January 1, 1182 |consort = yes |spouse = {{marriage|[[Emperor Takakura]]|1172|1181|end=died}} |issue = [[Emperor Antoku]] |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] |father = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] |mother = [[Taira no Tokiko]] |birth_date = 1155 |birth_place = |death_date = {{death date and age|1214|1|25|1155}} |death_place = Heian-kyō (Kyōto) |date of burial = |place of burial = |}} {{nihongo|'''Taira no Tokuko''' (Taira no Tokushi)|平 徳子||1155 – January 25, 1214}},[[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 333. later known as {{nihongo|'''Kenreimon-in'''|建礼門院|}}, was the daughter of [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[Taira no Tokiko]]. She was [[empress consort]] of [[Emperor Takakura]]. Tokuko was also the last Imperial survivor from the great naval [[battle of Dan-no-ura]].Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' pp. 652-678; [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 211-212. Her life became a compelling narrative which survives as both history and literature.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=978-0-231-13803-1 |pages=148–169}} ==In history== ===Daughter of an emperor=== Tokuko became the adopted daughter of {{nihongo|[[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]|後白河天皇,|''Go-Shirakawa-tennō''}}, the 77th emperor of Japan who reigned from 1155 through 1158. In 1171, when Tokuko was 17, the emperor had abdicated the throne and entered the Buddhist priesthood, taking the Buddhist name of Gyōshin. ===Consort of an emperor=== In 1172, Tokuko was married to Go-Shirakawa's fourth and only surviving son, [[Emperor Takakura]].Titsingh, pp. 188-190; Brown, pp. 326-327; [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki.'' pp. 205-208. Takakura was also her first cousin as both their mothers were half-sisters. The wedding was an arranged one, as to cement the alliance between the two co-fathers-in-law; Go-Shirakawa sponsored Kiyomori's rise as [[Daijō-daijin|Chancellor of the Realm]], while Kiyomori provided military and financial support to Go-Shirakawa. Tokuko and Takakura's son, [[Emperor Antoku|Prince Tokihito]], was born in 1178. Just a year later, Kiyomori launched a [[coup d'état]], sacking his political rivals from their posts, banishing them, and replacing them with his allies and clansmen. He even had Go-Shirakawa imprisoned; at this point they had fallen out of their political alliance.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |page=268,285}} [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|left|The ''Kenrei-mon'' (建礼門), one of the entrance gates to [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]]] In 1180, [[Emperor Takakura]] was forced to abdicate by Kiyomori; Prince Tokihito ascended the throne as [[Emperor Antoku]]. Now an empress dowager, Tokuko received the name Kenrei-mon In.Brown, p. 331; Titsingh, p. 424; Kitagawa, p. 764. In this period, the names of the several gates in the walls surrounding the imperial grounds refer not only to the wall-openings themselves; these names were also used to refer indirectly to a nearby residence of an empress whose husband had abdicated, or as an indirect way of referring to an empress dowager herself. For example, {{nihongo|Kenrei-mon In|建礼門院}}, whose official home, after the abdication and death of Emperor Takakura, was located near the Kenrei Gate. ===Mother of an emperor=== Tokuko, now Kenrei-mon In, was mother to Emperor Antoku. The child emperor reigned from 1180 through 1185.Titsingh, pp. 200-207; Brown, pp. 333-334; Varley, pp. 214-215. ===Survivor of Dan-no-ura=== Arguably, the most difficult moments in Kenreimon-In's life occurred near the close of the battle of Dan-no-ura, which took place near the southern tip of [[Honshū]] in [[Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|Shimonoseki]], [[Yamaguchi prefecture|Yamaguchi]]. * '''''[[Genryaku]] 2''''', on the 24th day of the 3rd month (April 25, 1185): The Taira and the Minamoto clashed for the last time.Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' p. 787; Titsingh, pp. 211-212. The Taira were defeated decisively. Many of the Taira samurai threw themselves into the waves rather than live to see their clan's ultimate defeat at the hands of the Minamoto. Antoku's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], Kiyomori's widow, leapt into the water with the young emperor clasped firmly in her arms.Kitagawa, pp. 676-677. Kenrei-mon In also tried to drown herself; but according to the conventionally accepted accounts, she was pulled out with a [[rake (tool)|rake]] by her long hair.Kitagawa, pp. 678; Titsingh, p. 212. ===Buddhist nun=== This sometime daughter, wife, and mother of emperors became a recluse in her later years. * '''''[[Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 1''''', on the 1st day of the 5th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In took the tonsure at [[Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)|Chōraku-ji]], a branch temple of [[Enryaku-ji]] on [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama]].Kitagawa, pp. 763-765. * '''''Bunji 1''''', on the 30th day of the 9th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In retreated further from the world when she moved to {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院}}, a Buddhist nunnery near the village of {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|lt=Ōhara|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, northeast of the Heian-kyō.Kitagawa, pp. 766-768, 787.[http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html ...Click for link to photos of Jakkō-in and Ōhara] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071024175701/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html |date=2007-10-24 }} * '''''Bunji 2''''', on the 20th day of the 4th month (1186): Gyōshin, the cloistered former-Emperor Go-Shirakawa, visited Kenrei-mon In at her rural retreat in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 769-779, 788. * '''''[[Kenkyū]] 2''''', in the 2nd month (1192): Kenrei-mon In dies in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 780-782, 788. [Although ''The Tale of the Heike'' gives 1192 as the year of Kenrei-mon In's death, the ''Gukanshō'' identifies 1213 as the year of her death -- cf. Brown, p. 333.] This once-pampered great lady is said to have composed this poem in her hermit's hut: {{poemquote| Did I ever dream That I would behold the moon Here on the mountain -- The moon that I used to view In the sky o'er the palace?Kitagawa, p. 772 }} ==In literature== Many stories and works of art depict this period in Japanese history, and it is through these sources that the life of Taira no Tokuko is best known. ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is the most famous of the sources from which we learn about this historical character, although many [[kabuki]] and [[bunraku]] plays reproduce events of the war as well. The central theme of the Heike story—and the mirrored theme of Taira no Tokuko's life story—is a demonstration of the Buddhist law of [[impermanence]]. The theme of impermanence (''mujō'') is captured in the opening passage:
The sound of the Gion Shōja bells echoes the impermanence of all things; the color of the sāla flowers reveals the truth that the prosperous must decline. The proud do not endure, they are like a dream on a spring night; the mighty fall at last, they are as dust before the wind.[[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike,''.
In this and other classic Japanese tales, the central figures are popularly well known, the major events are generally understood, and the stakes as they were understood at the time are conventionally accepted as elements in the foundation of Japanese culture. The accuracy of each of these historical records has become a compelling subject for further study; and some accounts have been shown to withstand close scrutiny, while other presumed “facts” have turned out to be inaccurate.Brown, pp. 385-386. In English-language literature, Tokuko's life and reign are depicted throughout the two-volume historical fiction narrative, ''White as Bone, Red as Blood'', by [[Cerridwen Fallingstar]], published in 2009 and 2011, respectively.[https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578027119 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Fox Sorceress''] (2009), and [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578073552 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Storm God''] (2011). ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichiro Ishida. (1979). ''The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō'.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0}} * Kitagawa, Hiroshi and Bruce T. Tsuchida. (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Palo Alto: [[Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-1803-2}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''. Stanford, California: [[Stanford University Press]]. * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'' (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --Click link to digitized, full-text copy of this book (in French)] * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''"A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa.'' New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Jakkō-in (Ōhara)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090914081019/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 |date=2009-09-14 }} * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105003911/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en |date=2008-01-05 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before= [[Fujiwara no Ikushi]] | title=[[Japanese empresses|Empress consort of Japan]] | after=[[Princess Sukeko]] | years=1172–1182}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} {{Redirect|Kenreimonin|the asteroid|5242 Kenreimonin}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty |name = Taira no Tokuko
{{lang|ja|平徳子}} |succession = [[Empress consort of Japan]] |image = Jakkoin by Mizuno Toshikata.jpg |reign = March 6, 1172 – January 1, 1182 |consort = yes |spouse = {{marriage|[[Emperor Takakura]]|1172|1181|end=died}} |issue = [[Emperor Antoku]] |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] |father = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] |mother = [[Taira no Tokiko]] |birth_date = 1155 |birth_place = |death_date = {{death date and age|1214|1|25|1155}} |death_place = Heian-kyō (Kyōto) |date of burial = |place of burial = |}} {{nihongo|'''Taira no Tokuko''' (Taira no Tokushi)|平 徳子||1155 – January 25, 1214}},[[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 333. later known as {{nihongo|'''Kenreimon-in'''|建礼門院|}}, was the daughter of [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[Taira no Tokiko]]. She was [[empress consort]] of [[Emperor Takakura]]. Tokuko was also the last Imperial survivor from the great naval [[battle of Dan-no-ura]].Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' pp. 652-678; [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 211-212. Her life became a compelling narrative which survives as both history and literature.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=978-0-231-13803-1 |pages=148–169}} ==In history== ===Daughter of an emperor=== Tokuko became the adopted daughter of {{nihongo|[[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]|後白河天皇,|''Go-Shirakawa-tennō''}}, the 77th emperor of Japan who reigned from 1155 through 1158. In 1171, when Tokuko was 17, the emperor had abdicated the throne and entered the Buddhist priesthood, taking the Buddhist name of Gyōshin. ===Consort of an emperor=== In 1172, Tokuko was married to Go-Shirakawa's fourth and only surviving son, [[Emperor Takakura]].Titsingh, pp. 188-190; Brown, pp. 326-327; [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki.'' pp. 205-208. Takakura was also her first cousin as both their mothers were half-sisters. The wedding was an arranged one, as to cement the alliance between the two co-fathers-in-law; Go-Shirakawa sponsored Kiyomori's rise as [[Daijō-daijin|Chancellor of the Realm]], while Kiyomori provided military and financial support to Go-Shirakawa. Tokuko and Takakura's son, [[Emperor Antoku|Prince Tokihito]], was born in 1178. Just a year later, Kiyomori launched a [[coup d'état]], sacking his political rivals from their posts, banishing them, and replacing them with his allies and clansmen. He even had Go-Shirakawa imprisoned; at this point they had fallen out of their political alliance.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |page=268,285}} [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|left|The ''Kenrei-mon'' (建礼門), one of the entrance gates to [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]]] In 1180, [[Emperor Takakura]] was forced to abdicate by Kiyomori; Prince Tokihito ascended the throne as [[Emperor Antoku]]. Now an empress dowager, Tokuko received the name Kenrei-mon In.Brown, p. 331; Titsingh, p. 424; Kitagawa, p. 764. In this period, the names of the several gates in the walls surrounding the imperial grounds refer not only to the wall-openings themselves; these names were also used to refer indirectly to a nearby residence of an empress whose husband had abdicated, or as an indirect way of referring to an empress dowager herself. For example, {{nihongo|Kenrei-mon In|建礼門院}}, whose official home, after the abdication and death of Emperor Takakura, was located near the Kenrei Gate. ===Mother of an emperor=== Tokuko, now Kenrei-mon In, was mother to Emperor Antoku. The child emperor reigned from 1180 through 1185.Titsingh, pp. 200-207; Brown, pp. 333-334; Varley, pp. 214-215. ===Survivor of Dan-no-ura=== Arguably, the most difficult moments in Kenreimon-In's life occurred near the close of the battle of Dan-no-ura, which took place near the southern tip of [[Honshū]] in [[Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|Shimonoseki]], [[Yamaguchi prefecture|Yamaguchi]]. * '''''[[Genryaku]] 2''''', on the 24th day of the 3rd month (April 25, 1185): The Taira and the Minamoto clashed for the last time.Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' p. 787; Titsingh, pp. 211-212. The Taira were defeated decisively. Many of the Taira samurai threw themselves into the waves rather than live to see their clan's ultimate defeat at the hands of the Minamoto. Antoku's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], Kiyomori's widow, leapt into the water with the young emperor clasped firmly in her arms.Kitagawa, pp. 676-677. Kenrei-mon In also tried to drown herself; but according to the conventionally accepted accounts, she was pulled out with a [[rake (tool)|rake]] by her long hair.Kitagawa, pp. 678; Titsingh, p. 212. ===Buddhist nun=== This sometime daughter, wife, and mother of emperors became a recluse in her later years. * '''''[[Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 1''''', on the 1st day of the 5th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In took the tonsure at [[Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)|Chōraku-ji]], a branch temple of [[Enryaku-ji]] on [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama]].Kitagawa, pp. 763-765. * '''''Bunji 1''''', on the 30th day of the 9th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In retreated further from the world when she moved to {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院}}, a Buddhist nunnery near the village of {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|lt=Ōhara|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, northeast of the Heian-kyō.Kitagawa, pp. 766-768, 787.[http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html ...Click for link to photos of Jakkō-in and Ōhara] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071024175701/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html |date=2007-10-24 }} * '''''Bunji 2''''', on the 20th day of the 4th month (1186): Gyōshin, the cloistered former-Emperor Go-Shirakawa, visited Kenrei-mon In at her rural retreat in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 769-779, 788. * '''''[[Kenkyū]] 2''''', in the 2nd month (1192): Kenrei-mon In dies in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 780-782, 788. [Although ''The Tale of the Heike'' gives 1192 as the year of Kenrei-mon In's death, the ''Gukanshō'' identifies 1213 as the year of her death -- cf. Brown, p. 333.] This once-pampered great lady is said to have composed this poem in her hermit's hut: {{poemquote| Did I ever dream That I would behold the moon Here on the mountain -- The moon that I used to view In the sky o'er the palace?Kitagawa, p. 772 }} ==In literature== Many stories and works of art depict this period in Japanese history, and it is through these sources that the life of Taira no Tokuko is best known. ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is the most famous of the sources from which we learn about this historical character, although many [[kabuki]] and [[bunraku]] plays reproduce events of the war as well. The central theme of the Heike story—and the mirrored theme of Taira no Tokuko's life story—is a demonstration of the Buddhist law of [[impermanence]]. The theme of impermanence (''mujō'') is captured in the opening passage:
The sound of the Gion Shōja bells echoes the impermanence of all things; the color of the sāla flowers reveals the truth that the prosperous must decline. The proud do not endure, they are like a dream on a spring night; the mighty fall at last, they are as dust before the wind.[[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike,''.
In this and other classic Japanese tales, the central figures are popularly well known, the major events are generally understood, and the stakes as they were understood at the time are conventionally accepted as elements in the foundation of Japanese culture. The accuracy of each of these historical records has become a compelling subject for further study; and some accounts have been shown to withstand close scrutiny, while other presumed “facts” have turned out to be inaccurate.Brown, pp. 385-386. In English-language literature, Tokuko's life and reign are depicted throughout the two-volume historical fiction narrative, ''White as Bone, Red as Blood'', by [[Cerridwen Fallingstar]], published in 2009 and 2011, respectively.[https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578027119 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Fox Sorceress''] (2009), and [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578073552 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Storm God''] (2011). ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichiro Ishida. (1979). ''The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō'.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0}} * Kitagawa, Hiroshi and Bruce T. Tsuchida. (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Palo Alto: [[Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-1803-2}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''. Stanford, California: [[Stanford University Press]]. * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'' (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --Click link to digitized, full-text copy of this book (in French)] * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''"A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa.'' New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Jakkō-in (Ōhara)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090914081019/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 |date=2009-09-14 }} * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105003911/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en |date=2008-01-05 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] * {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105003911/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en |date=2008-01-05 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{Redirect|Kenreimonin|the asteroid|5242 Kenreimonin}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty |name = Taira no Tokuko
{{lang|ja|平徳子}} |succession = [[Empress consort of Japan]] |image = Jakkoin by Mizuno Toshikata.jpg |reign = March 6, 1172 – January 1, 1182 |consort = yes |spouse = {{marriage|[[Emperor Takakura]]|1172|1181|end=died}} |issue = [[Emperor Antoku]] |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] |father = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] |mother = [[Taira no Tokiko]] |birth_date = 1155 |birth_place = |death_date = {{death date and age|1214|1|25|1155}} |death_place = Heian-kyō (Kyōto) |date of burial = |place of burial = |}} {{nihongo|'''Taira no Tokuko''' (Taira no Tokushi)|平 徳子||1155 – January 25, 1214}},[[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 333. later known as {{nihongo|'''Kenreimon-in'''|建礼門院|}}, was the daughter of [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[Taira no Tokiko]]. She was [[empress consort]] of [[Emperor Takakura]]. Tokuko was also the last Imperial survivor from the great naval [[battle of Dan-no-ura]].Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' pp. 652-678; [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 211-212. Her life became a compelling narrative which survives as both history and literature.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=978-0-231-13803-1 |pages=148–169}} ==In history== ===Daughter of an emperor=== Tokuko became the adopted daughter of {{nihongo|[[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]|後白河天皇,|''Go-Shirakawa-tennō''}}, the 77th emperor of Japan who reigned from 1155 through 1158. In 1171, when Tokuko was 17, the emperor had abdicated the throne and entered the Buddhist priesthood, taking the Buddhist name of Gyōshin. ===Consort of an emperor=== In 1172, Tokuko was married to Go-Shirakawa's fourth and only surviving son, [[Emperor Takakura]].Titsingh, pp. 188-190; Brown, pp. 326-327; [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki.'' pp. 205-208. Takakura was also her first cousin as both their mothers were half-sisters. The wedding was an arranged one, as to cement the alliance between the two co-fathers-in-law; Go-Shirakawa sponsored Kiyomori's rise as [[Daijō-daijin|Chancellor of the Realm]], while Kiyomori provided military and financial support to Go-Shirakawa. Tokuko and Takakura's son, [[Emperor Antoku|Prince Tokihito]], was born in 1178. Just a year later, Kiyomori launched a [[coup d'état]], sacking his political rivals from their posts, banishing them, and replacing them with his allies and clansmen. He even had Go-Shirakawa imprisoned; at this point they had fallen out of their political alliance.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |page=268,285}} [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|left|The ''Kenrei-mon'' (建礼門), one of the entrance gates to [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]]] In 1180, [[Emperor Takakura]] was forced to abdicate by Kiyomori; Prince Tokihito ascended the throne as [[Emperor Antoku]]. Now an empress dowager, Tokuko received the name Kenrei-mon In.Brown, p. 331; Titsingh, p. 424; Kitagawa, p. 764. In this period, the names of the several gates in the walls surrounding the imperial grounds refer not only to the wall-openings themselves; these names were also used to refer indirectly to a nearby residence of an empress whose husband had abdicated, or as an indirect way of referring to an empress dowager herself. For example, {{nihongo|Kenrei-mon In|建礼門院}}, whose official home, after the abdication and death of Emperor Takakura, was located near the Kenrei Gate. ===Mother of an emperor=== Tokuko, now Kenrei-mon In, was mother to Emperor Antoku. The child emperor reigned from 1180 through 1185.Titsingh, pp. 200-207; Brown, pp. 333-334; Varley, pp. 214-215. ===Survivor of Dan-no-ura=== Arguably, the most difficult moments in Kenreimon-In's life occurred near the close of the battle of Dan-no-ura, which took place near the southern tip of [[Honshū]] in [[Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|Shimonoseki]], [[Yamaguchi prefecture|Yamaguchi]]. * '''''[[Genryaku]] 2''''', on the 24th day of the 3rd month (April 25, 1185): The Taira and the Minamoto clashed for the last time.Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' p. 787; Titsingh, pp. 211-212. The Taira were defeated decisively. Many of the Taira samurai threw themselves into the waves rather than live to see their clan's ultimate defeat at the hands of the Minamoto. Antoku's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], Kiyomori's widow, leapt into the water with the young emperor clasped firmly in her arms.Kitagawa, pp. 676-677. Kenrei-mon In also tried to drown herself; but according to the conventionally accepted accounts, she was pulled out with a [[rake (tool)|rake]] by her long hair.Kitagawa, pp. 678; Titsingh, p. 212. ===Buddhist nun=== This sometime daughter, wife, and mother of emperors became a recluse in her later years. * '''''[[Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 1''''', on the 1st day of the 5th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In took the tonsure at [[Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)|Chōraku-ji]], a branch temple of [[Enryaku-ji]] on [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama]].Kitagawa, pp. 763-765. * '''''Bunji 1''''', on the 30th day of the 9th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In retreated further from the world when she moved to {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院}}, a Buddhist nunnery near the village of {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|lt=Ōhara|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, northeast of the Heian-kyō.Kitagawa, pp. 766-768, 787.[http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html ...Click for link to photos of Jakkō-in and Ōhara] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071024175701/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html |date=2007-10-24 }} * '''''Bunji 2''''', on the 20th day of the 4th month (1186): Gyōshin, the cloistered former-Emperor Go-Shirakawa, visited Kenrei-mon In at her rural retreat in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 769-779, 788. * '''''[[Kenkyū]] 2''''', in the 2nd month (1192): Kenrei-mon In dies in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 780-782, 788. [Although ''The Tale of the Heike'' gives 1192 as the year of Kenrei-mon In's death, the ''Gukanshō'' identifies 1213 as the year of her death -- cf. Brown, p. 333.] This once-pampered great lady is said to have composed this poem in her hermit's hut: {{poemquote| Did I ever dream That I would behold the moon Here on the mountain -- The moon that I used to view In the sky o'er the palace?Kitagawa, p. 772 }} ==In literature== Many stories and works of art depict this period in Japanese history, and it is through these sources that the life of Taira no Tokuko is best known. ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is the most famous of the sources from which we learn about this historical character, although many [[kabuki]] and [[bunraku]] plays reproduce events of the war as well. The central theme of the Heike story—and the mirrored theme of Taira no Tokuko's life story—is a demonstration of the Buddhist law of [[impermanence]]. The theme of impermanence (''mujō'') is captured in the opening passage:
The sound of the Gion Shōja bells echoes the impermanence of all things; the color of the sāla flowers reveals the truth that the prosperous must decline. The proud do not endure, they are like a dream on a spring night; the mighty fall at last, they are as dust before the wind.[[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike,''.
In this and other classic Japanese tales, the central figures are popularly well known, the major events are generally understood, and the stakes as they were understood at the time are conventionally accepted as elements in the foundation of Japanese culture. The accuracy of each of these historical records has become a compelling subject for further study; and some accounts have been shown to withstand close scrutiny, while other presumed “facts” have turned out to be inaccurate.Brown, pp. 385-386. In English-language literature, Tokuko's life and reign are depicted throughout the two-volume historical fiction narrative, ''White as Bone, Red as Blood'', by [[Cerridwen Fallingstar]], published in 2009 and 2011, respectively.[https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578027119 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Fox Sorceress''] (2009), and [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578073552 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Storm God''] (2011). ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichiro Ishida. (1979). ''The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō'.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0}} * Kitagawa, Hiroshi and Bruce T. Tsuchida. (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Palo Alto: [[Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-1803-2}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''. Stanford, California: [[Stanford University Press]]. * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'' (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --Click link to digitized, full-text copy of this book (in French)] * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''"A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa.'' New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Jakkō-in (Ōhara)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090914081019/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 |date=2009-09-14 }} * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105003911/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en |date=2008-01-05 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taira no Tokuko]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:Pages and Tokuko]]

''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Redirect|Kenreimonin|the asteroid|5242 Kenreimonin}} {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty |name = Taira no Tokuko
{{lang|ja|平徳子}} |succession = [[Empress consort of Japan]] |image = Jakkoin by Mizuno Toshikata.jpg |reign = March 6, 1172 – January 1, 1182 |consort = yes |spouse = {{marriage|[[Emperor Takakura]]|1172|1181|end=died}} |issue = [[Emperor Antoku]] |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] |father = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] |mother = [[Taira no Tokiko]] |birth_date = 1155 |birth_place = |death_date = {{death date and age|1214|1|25|1155}} |death_place = Heian-kyō (Kyōto) |date of burial = |place of burial = |}} {{nihongo|'''Taira no Tokuko''' (Taira no Tokushi)|平 徳子||1155 – January 25, 1214}},[[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 333. later known as {{nihongo|'''Kenreimon-in'''|建礼門院|}}, was the daughter of [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[Taira no Tokiko]]. She was [[empress consort]] of [[Emperor Takakura]]. Tokuko was also the last Imperial survivor from the great naval [[battle of Dan-no-ura]].Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' pp. 652-678; [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 211-212. Her life became a compelling narrative which survives as both history and literature.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=978-0-231-13803-1 |pages=148–169}} ==In history== ===Daughter of an emperor=== Tokuko became the adopted daughter of {{nihongo|[[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]|後白河天皇,|''Go-Shirakawa-tennō''}}, the 77th emperor of Japan who reigned from 1155 through 1158. In 1171, when Tokuko was 17, the emperor had abdicated the throne and entered the Buddhist priesthood, taking the Buddhist name of Gyōshin. ===Consort of an emperor=== In 1172, Tokuko was married to Go-Shirakawa's fourth and only surviving son, [[Emperor Takakura]].Titsingh, pp. 188-190; Brown, pp. 326-327; [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki.'' pp. 205-208. Takakura was also her first cousin as both their mothers were half-sisters. The wedding was an arranged one, as to cement the alliance between the two co-fathers-in-law; Go-Shirakawa sponsored Kiyomori's rise as [[Daijō-daijin|Chancellor of the Realm]], while Kiyomori provided military and financial support to Go-Shirakawa. Tokuko and Takakura's son, [[Emperor Antoku|Prince Tokihito]], was born in 1178. Just a year later, Kiyomori launched a [[coup d'état]], sacking his political rivals from their posts, banishing them, and replacing them with his allies and clansmen. He even had Go-Shirakawa imprisoned; at this point they had fallen out of their political alliance.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |page=268,285}} [[Image:Kyotopalace.jpg|thumb|left|The ''Kenrei-mon'' (建礼門), one of the entrance gates to [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]]] In 1180, [[Emperor Takakura]] was forced to abdicate by Kiyomori; Prince Tokihito ascended the throne as [[Emperor Antoku]]. Now an empress dowager, Tokuko received the name Kenrei-mon In.Brown, p. 331; Titsingh, p. 424; Kitagawa, p. 764. In this period, the names of the several gates in the walls surrounding the imperial grounds refer not only to the wall-openings themselves; these names were also used to refer indirectly to a nearby residence of an empress whose husband had abdicated, or as an indirect way of referring to an empress dowager herself. For example, {{nihongo|Kenrei-mon In|建礼門院}}, whose official home, after the abdication and death of Emperor Takakura, was located near the Kenrei Gate. ===Mother of an emperor=== Tokuko, now Kenrei-mon In, was mother to Emperor Antoku. The child emperor reigned from 1180 through 1185.Titsingh, pp. 200-207; Brown, pp. 333-334; Varley, pp. 214-215. ===Survivor of Dan-no-ura=== Arguably, the most difficult moments in Kenreimon-In's life occurred near the close of the battle of Dan-no-ura, which took place near the southern tip of [[Honshū]] in [[Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|Shimonoseki]], [[Yamaguchi prefecture|Yamaguchi]]. * '''''[[Genryaku]] 2''''', on the 24th day of the 3rd month (April 25, 1185): The Taira and the Minamoto clashed for the last time.Kitagawa, Hiroshi ''et al.'' (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike,'' p. 787; Titsingh, pp. 211-212. The Taira were defeated decisively. Many of the Taira samurai threw themselves into the waves rather than live to see their clan's ultimate defeat at the hands of the Minamoto. Antoku's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], Kiyomori's widow, leapt into the water with the young emperor clasped firmly in her arms.Kitagawa, pp. 676-677. Kenrei-mon In also tried to drown herself; but according to the conventionally accepted accounts, she was pulled out with a [[rake (tool)|rake]] by her long hair.Kitagawa, pp. 678; Titsingh, p. 212. ===Buddhist nun=== This sometime daughter, wife, and mother of emperors became a recluse in her later years. * '''''[[Bunji (era)|Bunji]] 1''''', on the 1st day of the 5th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In took the tonsure at [[Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)|Chōraku-ji]], a branch temple of [[Enryaku-ji]] on [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama]].Kitagawa, pp. 763-765. * '''''Bunji 1''''', on the 30th day of the 9th month (1185): Kenrei-mon In retreated further from the world when she moved to {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院}}, a Buddhist nunnery near the village of {{ill|Ōhara, Kyoto|lt=Ōhara|ja|大原 (京都市)}}, northeast of the Heian-kyō.Kitagawa, pp. 766-768, 787.[http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html ...Click for link to photos of Jakkō-in and Ōhara] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071024175701/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/ohara/jakkoin/ls_jakkoin.html |date=2007-10-24 }} * '''''Bunji 2''''', on the 20th day of the 4th month (1186): Gyōshin, the cloistered former-Emperor Go-Shirakawa, visited Kenrei-mon In at her rural retreat in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 769-779, 788. * '''''[[Kenkyū]] 2''''', in the 2nd month (1192): Kenrei-mon In dies in Ōhara.Kitagawa, pp. 780-782, 788. [Although ''The Tale of the Heike'' gives 1192 as the year of Kenrei-mon In's death, the ''Gukanshō'' identifies 1213 as the year of her death -- cf. Brown, p. 333.] This once-pampered great lady is said to have composed this poem in her hermit's hut: {{poemquote| Did I ever dream That I would behold the moon Here on the mountain -- The moon that I used to view In the sky o'er the palace?Kitagawa, p. 772 }} ==In literature== Many stories and works of art depict this period in Japanese history, and it is through these sources that the life of Taira no Tokuko is best known. ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is the most famous of the sources from which we learn about this historical character, although many [[kabuki]] and [[bunraku]] plays reproduce events of the war as well. The central theme of the Heike story—and the mirrored theme of Taira no Tokuko's life story—is a demonstration of the Buddhist law of [[impermanence]]. The theme of impermanence (''mujō'') is captured in the opening passage:
The sound of the Gion Shōja bells echoes the impermanence of all things; the color of the sāla flowers reveals the truth that the prosperous must decline. The proud do not endure, they are like a dream on a spring night; the mighty fall at last, they are as dust before the wind.[[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike,''.
In this and other classic Japanese tales, the central figures are popularly well known, the major events are generally understood, and the stakes as they were understood at the time are conventionally accepted as elements in the foundation of Japanese culture. The accuracy of each of these historical records has become a compelling subject for further study; and some accounts have been shown to withstand close scrutiny, while other presumed “facts” have turned out to be inaccurate.Brown, pp. 385-386. In English-language literature, Tokuko's life and reign are depicted throughout the two-volume historical fiction narrative, ''White as Bone, Red as Blood'', by [[Cerridwen Fallingstar]], published in 2009 and 2011, respectively.[https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578027119 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Fox Sorceress''] (2009), and [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578073552 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Storm God''] (2011). ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichiro Ishida. (1979). ''The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō'.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0}} * Kitagawa, Hiroshi and Bruce T. Tsuchida. (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Palo Alto: [[Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-1803-2}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''. Stanford, California: [[Stanford University Press]]. * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'' (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --Click link to digitized, full-text copy of this book (in French)] * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''"A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa.'' New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Jakkō-in (Ōhara)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090914081019/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?lang=en&InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000172 |date=2009-09-14 }} * [http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en Kyoto City Tourism and Culture Information System -- Chōraku-ji (Kyoto)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105003911/http://kaiwai.city.kyoto.jp/search/view_sight.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000136&lang=en |date=2008-01-05 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} Buddhist nuns]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taira no Tokuko]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taira no Tokuko]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taira no Tokuko]][[Category:kk]] In this and other classic Japanese tales, the central figures are popularly well known, the major events are generally understood, and the stakes as they were understood at the time are conventionally accepted as elements in the foundation of Japanese culture. The accuracy of each of these historical records has become a compelling subject for further study; and some accounts have been shown to withstand close scrutiny, while other presumed “facts” have turned out to be inaccurate.Brown, pp. 385-386. In English-language literature, Tokuko's life and reign are depicted throughout the two-volume historical fiction narrative, ''White as Bone, Red as Blood'', by [[Cerridwen Fallingstar]], published in 2009 and 2011, respectively.[https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578027119 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Fox Sorceress''] (2009), and [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0578073552 ''White as Bone, Red as Blood: The Storm God''] (2011). ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichiro Ishida. (1979). ''The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō'.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0}} * Kitagawa, Hiroshi and Bruce T. Tsuchida. (1975). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Tokyo: [[University of Tokyo Press]]. {{ISBN|0-86008-189-3}} * [[Helen Craig McCullough|McCullough, Helen Craig]]. (1988). ''The Tale of the Heike.'' Palo Alto: [[Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-1803-2}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''. Stanford, California: [[Stanford University Press]]. * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'' (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]].[https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --Click link to digitized, full-text copy of this book (in French)] * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul]]. (1980). ''"A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa.'' New York: [[Columbia University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4}} ==External links== {{Commons Category}} * [http://www.meijigakuin.ac.jp/~watson/heike/heike.html Meiji Gakuin University: ''Heike monogatari'' (in English)] * [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/heike/heike.html University of Virginia: ''Heike monogatari'' (in Japanese)] *
